md: ### The most famous circle theorem

Draw a circle. Pick two points $A$ and $B$ on it — they form a chord. Now pick a third point $P$ on the circle. The angle $\angle APB$, made by the chords $PA$ and $PB$, is called an **inscribed angle** standing on the arc $AB$.
Here's the magic: **the inscribed angle is always exactly half the central angle on the same arc**.

Drag $P$ around the circle in the widget below. The central angle $\angle AOB$ (green arcs at the center) stays fixed. The inscribed angle $\angle APB$ (red arcs at $P$) is exactly half of it — at every position of $P$.

blocks.title: Why this is wild
blocks.body:
$P$ can be anywhere on the major arc — top of the circle, side of the circle, anywhere — and the inscribed angle does **not** change. That's not how it usually works in geometry: most angles depend on where the vertex sits. But on a circle, all viewing points on the same arc see chord $AB$ at the same angle.

It's also exactly *half* the central angle. The number 2 sneaks in because of the geometry of isosceles triangles (proof below).

md: ### Sketch of the proof
Connect $P$ to the center $O$. This creates two isosceles triangles ($OA = OP$ = radius, and $OB = OP$ = radius). In each isosceles triangle the base angles are equal.

If $\angle OPA = \alpha$ and $\angle OPB = \beta$, then the inscribed angle is $\alpha + \beta$. The two corresponding central angles (the exterior angles of each isosceles triangle at $O$) are $2\alpha$ and $2\beta$, summing to $2(\alpha + \beta)$.
So central angle $= 2 \cdot$ inscribed angle. ∎

blocks.title: Three special cases
blocks.body:
**1. Same segment.** Two inscribed angles standing on the same chord, on the same side of the chord, are equal (both are half the same central angle).

**2. Diameter ⇒ right angle.** When $AB$ is a diameter, the central angle is $180°$, so the inscribed angle is $90°$. This is **Thales' Theorem** — covered separately.

**3. Opposite arcs.** If $P$ is on the *other* side of chord $AB$ (the minor arc instead of the major one), the inscribed angle there is supplementary to the one on the major arc — i.e., they sum to $180°$. This is why opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to $180°$.

md: ### Worked example
Chord $AB$ subtends a central angle of $80°$. A point $P$ is on the major arc. What is $\angle APB$?
**By the Inscribed Angle Theorem:** $\angle APB = 80°/2 = 40°$.
If instead $P$ were on the minor arc (the other side), $\angle APB$ would be $180° - 40° = 140°$ (supplementary to the major-arc inscribed angle).
**Reverse problem.** A chord $AB$ is viewed from a point $P$ on a circle at an angle of $25°$. What's the central angle? **Answer:** $50°$ (the central angle is double).
### Why students should care
This theorem is the key to **every** problem about angles in circles. With it you can:
- Solve for unknown angles in figures involving chords and arcs.
- Prove that four points are concyclic (lie on the same circle).
- Derive the cyclic quadrilateral theorem (next lesson).
- Locate points seeing a fixed segment at a fixed angle — they all lie on a circular arc.
It's so fundamental that olympiad geometry essentially *runs* on inscribed-angle chasing.
